title: Principled to a fault: India’s foreign policy trap
gist: The Modi-Trump fallout — sparked by India refusing Trump credit for the May 2025 India-Pakistan ceasefire — is the latest episode of New Delhi choosing principle over pragmatism, a pattern dating to Nehru. The author argues India's idealism has repeatedly cost it strategically (1962 China war, Israel, Myanmar) and that it now needs a more pragmatic, less preachy foreign policy because principle flows from power, which India still lacks.

insights.claim: The India-US downturn traces to New Delhi insisting the May 2025 four-day India-Pakistan conflict ended without third-party involvement — refusing to give Trump the ceasefire 'win' Pakistan happily granted (even nominating him for a Nobel) — consistent with India's long-standing opposition to third-party mediation on Kashmir.
insights.speaker: Chietigj Bajpaee
insights.so_what: India's principled refusal carried real cost; the episode is the root of subsequent tariff and sanctions friction — watch Kashmir-mediation flashpoints as US-India relationship risk.
insights.quote: This became the "original sin" in the bilateral relationship.
insights.locator: The roots of the downturn with Washington

insights.claim: The 'original sin' was followed by higher US tariffs on India, secondary sanctions for its Russian crude purchases, and a deepening Washington-Islamabad relationship fuelled by Pakistan's mediation role in the Iran conflict.
insights.speaker: Chietigj Bajpaee
insights.so_what: Concrete consequences (tariffs, Russian-oil sanctions, Pakistan tilt) make the relationship deterioration tradeable for India-exposed assets and energy-import flows.
insights.locator: It was soon followed by the Trump administration's imposition

insights.claim: Nehru took the Pakistan conflict to the UN (internationalising the Kashmir dispute), advocated for Communist China's UN Security Council seat while downplaying the China border threat, and this idealism culminated in the disastrous 1962 war.
insights.speaker: Chietigj Bajpaee
insights.so_what: The historical pattern shows idealism repeatedly produced strategic misjudgement; useful base rate for assessing whether current Indian policy will course-correct slowly or only after a shock.

insights.claim: India's staunch support for the Palestinian cause undermined relations with Israel, and its vocal condemnation of Myanmar's junta after 1988/1990 pushed the regime into China's arms — both later course-corrected (Israel now a key partner; junta chief Min Aung Hlaing hosted in April).
insights.speaker: Chietigj Bajpaee
insights.so_what: India's corrections come slowly or after shocks; the lag between principle and pragmatism is the structural inefficiency to price in Indian strategic positioning.
insights.locator: New Delhi's position on the Israeli–Palestinian conflict

insights.claim: The Quad has lost momentum and the US Department of War's move to drop 'Indo' from Indo-Pacific Command, though more symbolic than substantive, reflects a relative downgrade of India's position in Washington's Asia strategy.
insights.speaker: Chietigj Bajpaee
insights.so_what: Symbolic downgrades plus Quad drift signal reduced US strategic weighting of India — relevant to defence-cooperation and trade-agreement timelines.

insights.claim: Outstanding frictions include delays in a bilateral trade agreement, deaths of Indian seafarers from US strikes on commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, and the expiry of a US sanctions waiver on India's Russian oil purchases.
insights.speaker: Chietigj Bajpaee
insights.so_what: The Russian-oil waiver expiry and stalled trade deal are concrete near-term catalysts for India's import costs and US-India trade exposure.
insights.locator: The relationship is facing several thorns

insights.claim: India being singled out for Russian crude purchases while China — also a major buyer — was not targeted to the same degree reflects India's lack of strategic indispensability, not merely a double standard.
insights.speaker: Chietigj Bajpaee
insights.so_what: India's lower strategic leverage vs China means it absorbs more sanctions pressure for the same behaviour; a structural asymmetry for India-Russia energy flows.

insights.claim: India needs a more pragmatic and less preachy foreign policy in a world where values no longer carry the same currency.
insights.speaker: Chietigj Bajpaee
insights.so_what: Expect (and watch for) Indian pragmatic course-correction; transactional flattery of Trump at the G7 suggests the shift has already begun.
insights.quote: India needs a more pragmatic and less preachy foreign policy in a world where values no longer carry the same currency.
